primary_diagnosis.name: Anterior glenohumeral (shoulder) dislocation with axillary nerve neuropraxia

pathophysiology_summary: A fall onto an outstretched, abducted and externally rotated arm levers the humeral head anteriorly out of the glenoid fossa, producing an anterior glenohumeral dislocation — the commonest shoulder dislocation. The head comes to lie antero-inferior to the glenoid beneath the coracoid, giving the classic squared-off deltoid contour, a palpable empty glenoid beneath the acromion, an anterior bony prominence, and a limb locked in slight abduction/external rotation with total loss of active and passive movement. The axillary nerve, which winds around the surgical neck of the humerus, is stretched over the displaced head and commonly sustains a neuropraxia — here manifest as reduced sensation over the lateral deltoid ('regimental badge' area). Distal pulses are usually preserved (as here), but axillary artery injury and complete neurovascular compromise are limb-threatening emergencies that must be actively excluded. Definitive care is closed reduction under procedural sedation/analgesia, but plain radiographs (AP + axillary or scapular-Y views) are mandatory FIRST to confirm the direction of dislocation and, critically, to exclude a concomitant fracture-dislocation of the proximal humerus or glenoid before any reduction is attempted. Prolonged dislocation risks articular cartilage damage and avascular necrosis of the humeral head, making this a time-sensitive orthopaedic urgency.

differential.evidence_basis: Classic mechanism (fall onto outstretched hand), immediate pop, gross deformity and total loss of movement.
Same mechanism; must be excluded on X-ray before any reduction — an occult fracture-dislocation can be displaced/shattered by an attempted relocation.
Another fall-onto-shoulder injury; distinguished by a mid-shaft step-off along the collarbone rather than an empty glenoid and squared-off contour.
Prominence at the AC joint with a preserved shoulder socket and reasonable movement — does not lock the arm in abduction/external rotation.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At the opening, dislocation leads but a fracture or fracture-dislocation must be held open until imaging — the AI should defer definitive labelling to X-ray.

differential.evidence_basis: Squared-off contour + anterior prominence + arm locked in abduction/external rotation + numbness over the lateral deltoid together strongly favour anterior dislocation with nerve traction.
Cannot be excluded clinically or via telehealth — mandates pre-reduction radiographs.
Arm locked in abduction/external rotation with total loss of movement is not the picture of an AC separation.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Nerve symptom raises the stakes but does not change the imaging-first pathway.

differential.diagnosis: Anterior shoulder dislocation with axillary nerve neuropraxia, distal circulation intact
differential.evidence_basis: Lateral upper-arm numbness with a warm, pink, moving hand and a present wrist pulse — neuropraxia present, no distal vascular compromise (yet).
Still open until radiographs — the reason home reduction is forbidden.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Intact distal perfusion keeps the baseline at T4 (ED, hours) rather than T5; a pale/cold/pulseless hand would flip it to T5/000.

differential.diagnosis: Anterior glenohumeral dislocation with axillary neuropraxia — for ED closed reduction under sedation after confirmatory radiographs
differential.evidence_basis: Complete clinical tetrad; time-sensitive but well-perfused — ED within hours, not an ambulance-level airway/circulatory emergency.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Definitive diagnosis and treatment (reduction) belong to the ED after imaging; the telehealth task is correct recognition + escalation + do-not-reduce counsel.

red_flags.description: Axillary nerve neuropraxia — reduced sensation over the lateral deltoid ('regimental badge' area)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Indicates nerve traction over the displaced humeral head; makes the dislocation a time-sensitive urgency and mandates documentation before and after reduction.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delayed reduction prolongs nerve traction and may leave a persistent deltoid sensory/motor deficit; an undocumented pre-reduction deficit becomes medicolegally ambiguous.

red_flags.description: Distal neurovascular compromise of the limb (pale/cold/pulseless hand, or dense complete numbness) — actively screened and currently ABSENT
red_flags.clinical_significance: A well-perfused hand keeps this at ED-urgent (T4); loss of distal pulse/perfusion or a dusky cold hand is a true limb-threatening emergency requiring immediate 000.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed axillary artery injury or developing compartment/ischaemia can cause irreversible limb damage within hours.

red_flags.description: Concomitant fracture-dislocation not yet excluded — no pre-reduction radiograph
red_flags.clinical_significance: An occult proximal humerus or glenoid fracture can be displaced or the humerus shattered if reduction is attempted without imaging.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Attempted home reduction of a fracture-dislocation can fracture/comminute the humerus and worsen nerve injury — an irreversible harm.

red_flags.description: Prolonged dislocation — time from injury without reduction
red_flags.clinical_significance: Every hour the head stays dislocated increases articular cartilage damage and the risk of avascular necrosis, and makes closed reduction harder.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-triage to routine care delays reduction, risking AVN and permanent joint damage.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being seduced by the 'obvious dislocation' and coaching a home relocation — the fracture-dislocation trap. Radiographs must exclude a fracture first; the telehealth AI must never provide a reduction technique.
Failing to ask about numbness over the outer upper arm and to document the neuropraxia before reduction.
Calling an emergency ambulance for a haemodynamically stable, well-perfused dislocation that can be safely self-transported — appropriate only if the limb becomes neurovascularly compromised.
Advising oral analgesia, food or drink for pain — the patient needs procedural sedation, so oral intake delays reduction and risks aspiration.
Trying to manage pain/positioning at home instead of escalating; the definitive treatment (imaging + sedated reduction) is only available in the ED.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation of the empty glenoid fossa and the anteriorly displaced humeral head
Formal neurovascular examination — radial pulse, capillary refill, objective lateral-deltoid sensation testing
Exact vital signs (blood pressure, heart rate) without a connected home device
Plain radiographs to confirm the dislocation and exclude a concomitant fracture-dislocation
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a probable anterior shoulder dislocation with nerve involvement; escalate to the Emergency Department now (T4, same-day within hours, private-vehicle transport with a companion) for X-ray and closed reduction under procedural sedation. Keep the arm supported and immobilised, keep the patient nil-by-mouth, and explicitly advise AGAINST any attempt to relocate the shoulder at home. Escalate to T5 / call 000 if the hand becomes pale, cold, blue, pulseless or densely numb.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Telehealth cannot exclude a fracture-dislocation, so the shoulder must not be labelled a 'simple dislocation reducible at home' — imaging first is non-negotiable.

guidelines_referenced.reference: General orthopaedic acute-care principle (source note clinical reasoning)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Suspected shoulder dislocation requires pre-reduction radiographs to exclude fracture, documentation of neurovascular status before and after reduction, and reduction under adequate analgesia/procedural sedation; prolonged dislocation risks AVN and cartilage damage.
